perturbation.original_text: Maimon agrees during the term of this Agreement not to accept work or enter into a contract or accept an obligation inconsistent or incompatible with Maimon's obligations under this Agreement or with the scope of services to be rendered for the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Maimon agrees during the term of this Agreement to accept any work or enter into any contract or accept any obligation, as long as it does not demonstrably harm the Company, at Maimon's sole discretion.
perturbation.explanation: This clause is now overly broad and discretionary, potentially conflicting with Maimon's fiduciary duty to the Company and creating ambiguity about acceptable outside activities.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York law imposes a duty of loyalty and care on corporate officers. By allowing the CEO to engage in potentially conflicting activities at his sole discretion, the modified text weakens the fiduciary duty owed to the corporation, potentially violating Section 717 of the NY Business Corporation Law.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 4 Business Corporation ARTICLE 7 Directors and Officers previous SECTION 716 Removal of officers up ARTICLE 7 Directors and Officers next SECTION 718 List of directors and officers This entry was published on 2017-05-05 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-11-28 2014-12-05 2015-01-09 2015-04-24 2015-11-27 2016-01-22 2016-03-11 2016-08-26 2016-11-25 2017-04-28 2017-05-05 2017-09-15 2018-01-05 2018-04-27 2018-10-05 2019-10-25 2019-11-29 2020-01-31 2020-02-07 2020-06-19 2020-06-26 2020-07-03 2021-04-09 2021-04-23 2021-11-12 2021-12-03 2022-02-04 2022-07-29 2023-01-06 2023-12-15 2024-03-08 2024-06-14 2024-07-26 Share Facebook Email SECTION 717 Duty of directors Business Corporation (BSC) CHAPTER 4, ARTICLE 7 § 717. Duty of directors. (a) A director shall perform his duties as a director, including his duties as a member of any committee of the board upon which he may serve, in good faith and with that degree of care which an ordinarily prudent person in a like position would use under similar circumstances. In performing his duties, a director shall be entitled to rely on information, opinions, reports or statements including financial statements and other financial data, in each case prepared or presented by: (1) one or more officers or employees of the corporation or of any other corporation of which at least fifty percentum of the outstanding shares of stock entitling the holders thereof to vote for the election of directors is owned directly or indirectly by the corporation, whom the director believes to be reliable and competent in the matters presented, (2) counsel, public accountants or other persons as to matters which the director believes to be within such person's professional or expert competence, or (3) a committee of the board upon which he does not serve, duly designated in accordance with a provision of the certificate of incorporation or the by-laws, as to matters within its designated authority, which committee the director believes to merit confidence, so long as in so relying he shall be acting in good faith and with such degree of care, but he shall not be considered to be acting in good faith if he has knowledge concerning the matter in question that would cause such reliance to be unwarranted. A person who so performs his duties shall have no liability by reason of being or having been a director of the corporation. (b) In taking action, including, without limitation, action which may involve or relate to a change or potential change in the control of the corporation, a director shall be entitled to consider, without limitation, (1) both the long-term and the short-term interests o
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts the duty of loyalty and care required of directors under New York law, specifically Section 717 of the NY Business Corporation Law. The original text prevented Maimon from accepting work that conflicts with his obligations to the company. The changed text allows Maimon to accept any work as long as it doesn't demonstrably harm the company, giving him sole discretion. This directly weakens the fiduciary duty and potentially violates Section 717, which requires directors to act in good faith and with the degree of care an ordinarily prudent person would use. The snippet provided indicates that a director must perform their duties 'in good faith and with that degree of care which an ordinarily prudent person in a like position would use under similar circumstances.' Granting sole discretion to accept potentially conflicting work undermines this duty.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information. Maimon recognizes and acknowledges that by reason of his retention by and service to the Company, Maimon will have access to certain confidential and proprietary information relating to the Company's business, which may include, but is not limited to, trade secrets, trade "know-how," product development techniques and plans, formulas, customer lists and addresses, financing services, funding programs, cost and pricing information, marketing and sales techniques, strategy and programs, computer programs and software and financial information (collectively referred to as "Confidential Information").
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information. Maimon recognizes and acknowledges that by reason of his retention by and service to the Company, Maimon will have access to certain confidential and proprietary information relating to the Company's business, which may include, but is not limited to, product development techniques and plans, formulas, customer lists and addresses, financing services, funding programs, cost and pricing information, marketing and sales techniques, strategy and programs, computer programs and software and financial information (collectively referred to as "Confidential Information").
perturbation.explanation: By removing "trade secrets, trade 'know-how'" from the definition of Confidential Information, the agreement now offers less protection than trade secret law provides, weakening protections for Intellectual Property.
perturbation.law_explanation: By omitting 'trade secrets' and 'trade know-how' from the definition, the contract fails to adequately protect information that would otherwise be protected under New York's trade secret laws. This potentially limits the company's ability to pursue legal action for misappropriation of such information because the contract does not explicitly identify it as confidential.

perturbation.justification: The 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' arises because the changed contract omits 'trade secrets' and 'trade know-how' from the definition of 'Confidential Information.' This omission, as explained, weakens the protection of Intellectual Property, potentially limiting the company's ability to pursue legal action for misappropriation. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight the contradiction, stating the contract now offers less protection than trade secret law provides and fails to adequately protect information that would otherwise be protected. The original contract included these terms, aligning with common legal definitions of confidential information and trade secrets. By removing them, the changed text misaligns with established legal terminology, making the contract potentially unenforceable in regards to those specific types of information. Therefore, a contradiction exists because the contract's definition deviates from legally recognized definitions related to trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: If Maimon should become unable to serve as CEO, or should fail to perform any of the obligations hereunder for any cause including death or disability, always in the sole judgment and decision of the Company, then the Company shall have the right to terminate this agreement on five days prior written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: If Maimon should become unable to serve as CEO, or should fail to perform any of the obligations hereunder for any cause, always in the sole judgment and decision of the Company, then the Company shall have the right to terminate this agreement on five days prior written notice.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'including death or disability' from the list of causes allows for termination for disability without consideration of protections under the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA).
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification allows for termination based on disability without any consideration of reasonable accommodations as required by the ADA. By removing 'including death or disability', it implies that any inability to perform, regardless of whether it can be accommodated, is grounds for termination, violating the ADA's protections against discrimination based on disability.

Table of contents Law, Regulations & Standards When we talk about what the ADA requires on ADA.gov, we are usually referring to two sources. First, the text of the ADA, also referred to as the ADA statute, passed by Congress in 1990 and later amended. Second, regulations developed by the Department of Justice that state/local governments and many businesses must follow to ensure that they do not discriminate against people with disabilities. Regulations Regulations under the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) explain the rights of people with disabilities and the obligations of those covered by the laws. Learn more Design Standards The ADA Standards for Accessible Design say what is required for a building or facility to be physically accessible to people with disabilities. Learn more Or, go straight to any of the following by clicking any of the links below. The Americans with Disabilities Act This is the law that protects the civil rights of people with disabilities in many aspects of public life. Title II Regulations State and Local Governments The Title II Regulations explains the rights of people with disabilities and the obligations of state and local governments. Title III Regulations Public Accommodations and Commercial Facilities The Title III Regulations explains the rights of people with disabilities and the obligations of businesses, nonprofit organizations, and commercial facilities. Resources Guides and primers curate information for people with disabilities, state and local governments, and businesses. A Guide to Disability Rights Laws A brief overview of ten Federal laws that protect the rights of people with disabilities and the Federal agencies to contact for more information. A Primer for State and Local Governments This document provides general guidance to assist State and local governments in understanding and complying with the ADA's requirements. A Primer for Small Business This document provides general guidance to assist small businesses in understanding and complying with the ADA's requirements. Proposed Changes to Our Regulations In the Fall 2024 Unified Agenda , DOJ announced that it plans to issue new ADA regulations on the following topics: The Public Right of Way Equipment and Furniture Federally Conducted Programs What is the Unified Agenda? The Unified Agenda provides information about federal agencies’ regulatory priorities and the specific regulations that they plan to issue in the short and long term. perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes 'including death or disability' from the list of causes for termination, which raises concerns about compliance with the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA). The ADA prohibits discrimination based on disability, and the original contract's explicit mention of 'death or disability' provided a degree of protection. By removing this phrase, the contract potentially allows the company to terminate an employee based on disability without considering reasonable accommodations, contradicting the ADA's protections. Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that the ADA protects the civil rights of people with disabilities in many aspects of public life. Therefore, removing the clause creates a legal contradiction.
